title: Sarga 89
summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: Themes: self-knowledge, devotion, freedom from attachment. The passage moves from “Having heard the origin of the Kimpurushas, Lakshmana and Bharata then both exclaimed "This is wonderful!" to Rama, the lord of people.” through “Hearing her wonderful words, the son of the Moon, filled with desire, indeed rejoiced and enjoyed himself with her.” through “Having heard the bewildered words of that royal sage, Budha replied with auspicious words, consoling him with a supreme voice.”

Themes: self-knowledge, mind and meditation, virtue and right conduct. The passage moves from “Your servants were killed by a great shower of stones, and you were sleeping in the hermitage, afflicted by the fear of wind and rain.” through “As the king of kings spoke thus, Budha, in an exceedingly wonderful and consoling manner, then said, "May your dwelling here be pleasing to you."” through “As soon as the very powerful son, who resembled Budha in complexion, was born, Ila of beautiful hips placed him in his father Budha's hand.”
Themes: virtue and right conduct. But the wise Budha, after a year, entertained Ila, who had become a man again, with righteous stories. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये…

verses.sanskrit: श्रुत्वा किम्पुरुषोत्पत्तिं लक्ष्मणो भरतस्तदा । आश्चर्यमिति चाब्रूतामुभौ रामं जनेश्वरम् ।। 7.89.1 ।।
verses.translation: Having heard the origin of the Kimpurushas, Lakshmana and Bharata then both exclaimed "This is wonderful!" to Rama, the lord of people.

verses.sanskrit: अथ रामः कथामेतां भूय एव महायशाः । कथयामास धर्मात्मा प्रजापतिसुतस्य वै ।। 7.89.2 ।।
verses.translation: Then, the greatly renowned and righteous-souled Rama indeed narrated this story again, concerning the son of Prajapati.

verses.sanskrit: सर्वास्ता विद्रुता दृष्ट्वा किन्नरीर्ऋषिसत्तमः । उवाच रूपसम्पन्नां तां स्त्रियं प्रहसन्निव ।। 7.89.3 ।।
verses.translation: Seeing all the Kinnaris flee, the best among sages, Budha, spoke to that beautiful woman as if laughing.

verses.sanskrit: सोमस्याहं सुदयितः सुतः सुरुचिरानने । भजस्व मां वरारोहे भक्त्या स्निग्धेन चक्षुषा ।। 7.89.4 ।।
verses.translation: Budha declared himself to be the beloved son of Soma and urged the beautiful woman to love him with devoted and tender eyes.

verses.sanskrit: तस्य तद्वचनं श्रुत्वा शून्ये स्वजनवर्जिते । इला सुरुचिरप्रख्यं प्रत्युवाच महाग्रहम् ।। 7.89.5 ।।
verses.translation: Hearing his words in that desolate place, devoid of her own people, Ila replied to the radiant Budha, the great planet.

verses.sanskrit: अहं कामचरी सौम्य तवास्मि वशवर्तिनी । प्रशाधि मां सोमसुत यथेच्छसि तथा कुरु ।। 7.89.6 ।।
verses.translation: Ila declared herself to be free-willed but now submissive to him, asking the gentle son of Soma to command her and do as he pleased.

verses.sanskrit: तस्यास्तदद्भुतप्रख्यं श्रुत्वा हर्षमुपागतः । स वै कामी सह तया रेमे चन्द्रमसः सुतः ।। 7.89.7 ।।
verses.translation: Hearing her wonderful words, the son of the Moon, filled with desire, indeed rejoiced and enjoyed himself with her.

verses.sanskrit: बुधस्य माधवो मासस्तामिलां रुचिराननाम् । गतो रमयतो ऽत्यर्थं क्षणवत्तस्य कामिनः ।। 7.89.8 ।।
verses.translation: For Budha, the lustful one, the month of Madhava passed like a moment while he was exceedingly enjoying with Tamila of the beautiful face.

verses.sanskrit: अथ मासे तु सम्पूर्णे पूर्णेन्दुसदृशाननः । प्रजापतिसुतः श्रीमाञ्छयने प्रत्यबुध्यत ।। 7.89.9 ।।
verses.translation: Then, when the month was complete, the glorious son of Prajapati, whose face was like the full moon, awoke on his bed.

verses.sanskrit: सो ऽपश्यत्सोमजं तत्र तपन्तं सलिलाशये । ऊर्ध्वबाहुं निरालम्बं तं राजा प्रत्यभाषत ।। 7.89.10 ।।
verses.translation: The king (Pururavas) saw Budha, the son of Soma, performing severe penance there in the water, with his arms raised and without support, and then the king spoke to him.

verses.sanskrit: भगवन्पर्वतं दुर्गं प्रविष्टो ऽस्मि सहानुगः । न च पश्यामि तत्सैन्यं क्व नु ते मामका गताः ।। 7.89.11 ।।
verses.translation: The king addressed Budha, saying, "O venerable one, I entered this difficult mountain with my followers, but I do not see my army; where have my people gone?"

verses.sanskrit: तच्छ्रुत्वा तस्य राजर्षेर्नष्टसञ्ज्ञस्य भाषितम् । प्रत्युवाच शुभं वाक्यं सान्त्वयन्परया गिरा ।। 7.89.12 ।।
verses.translation: Having heard the bewildered words of that royal sage, Budha replied with auspicious words, consoling him with a supreme voice.

verses.sanskrit: अश्मवर्षेण महता भृत्यास्ते विनिपातिताः । त्वं चाश्रमपदे सुप्तो वातवर्षभयार्दितः ।। 7.89.13 ।।
verses.translation: Your servants were killed by a great shower of stones, and you were sleeping in the hermitage, afflicted by the fear of wind and rain.

verses.sanskrit: समाश्वसिहि भद्रं ते निर्भयो विगतज्वरः । फलमूलाशनो वीर निवसेह यथासुखम् ।। 7.89.14 ।।
verses.translation: Be comforted, may good fortune be with you; O hero, reside here fearlessly and free from anxiety, subsisting on fruits and roots as comfortably as you wish.

verses.sanskrit: स राजा तेन वाक्येन प्रत्याश्वस्तो महामतिः । प्रत्युवाच ततो वाक्यं दीनो भृत्यक्षयाद् भृशम् ।। 7.89.15 ।।
verses.translation: That great-minded king, though reassured by those words, then replied with great distress due to the loss of his servants.

verses.sanskrit: त्यक्ष्याम्यहं स्वकं राज्यं नाहं भृत्यैर्विनाकृतः । वर्तयेयं क्षणं ब्रह्मन्समनुज्ञातुमर्हसि ।। 7.89.16 ।।
verses.translation: O Brahmana, I will abandon my own kingdom, for I cannot live even for a moment without my servants; you ought to permit me to do so.

verses.sanskrit: सुतो धर्मपरो ब्रह्मञ्ज्येष्ठो मम महायशाः । शशबिन्दुरिति ख्यातः स मे राज्यं प्रपत्स्यते ।। 7.89.17 ।।
verses.translation: O Brahmana, my eldest son, who is devoted to righteousness and very famous by the name Shashabindu, will succeed to my kingdom.

verses.sanskrit: न हि शक्ष्याम्यहं हित्वा भृत्यदारान्सुखान्वितान् । प्रतिवक्तुं महातेजः किञ्चिदप्यशुभं वचः ।। 7.89.18 ।।
verses.translation: O greatly effulgent one, I am indeed unable to utter even a single inauspicious word in reply, having abandoned my comfortable servants and wives.

verses.sanskrit: तथा ब्रुवति राजेन्द्रे बुधः परममद्भुतम् । सान्त्वपूर्वमथोवाच वासस्त इह रोचताम् ।। 7.89.19 ।।
verses.translation: As the king of kings spoke thus, Budha, in an exceedingly wonderful and consoling manner, then said, "May your dwelling here be pleasing to you."

verses.sanskrit: न सन्तापस्त्वया कार्यः कार्दमेय महाबल । संवत्सरोषितस्येह कारयिष्यामि ते हितम् ।। 7.89.20 ।।
verses.translation: O mighty son of Kardama, you should not feel any sorrow, for I shall ensure your well-being here after you have resided for a year.

verses.sanskrit: तस्य तद्वचनं श्रुत्वा बुधस्याक्लिष्टकर्मणः । वासाय विदधे बुद्धिं यदुक्तं ब्रह्मवादिना ।। 7.89.21 ।।
verses.translation: Having heard that word from Budha, whose deeds were untiring and who spoke of Brahman, Ila resolved in his mind to dwell there as instructed.

verses.sanskrit: मासं स स्त्री तदा भूत्वा रमयत्यनिशं शुदा । मासं पुरुषभावेन धर्मबुद्धिं चकार सः ।। 7.89.22 ।।
verses.translation: For one month, he became a woman and constantly delighted happily, and for the next month, he assumed the state of a man and focused his mind on righteousness.

verses.sanskrit: ततः सा नवमे मासि इला सोमसुतात्सुतम् । जनयामास सुश्रोणी पुरूरवसमूर्जितम् ।। 7.89.23 ।।
verses.translation: Then, in the ninth month, Ila, who had beautiful hips, gave birth to a mighty son named Pururavas from Budha, the son of Soma.

verses.sanskrit: जातमात्रं तु सुश्रोणी पितुर्हस्ते न्यवेशयत् । बुधस्य समवर्णभमिला पुत्रं महाबलम् ।। 7.89.24 ।।
verses.translation: As soon as the very powerful son, who resembled Budha in complexion, was born, Ila of beautiful hips placed him in his father Budha's hand.

verses.sanskrit: बुधस्तु पुरुषीभूतं स वै संवत्सरान्तरम् । कथाभी रमयामास धर्मयुक्ताभिरात्मवान् ।। 7.89.25 ।।
verses.translation: But the wise Budha, after a year, entertained Ila, who had become a man again, with righteous stories. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये श्रीमदुत्तरकाण्डे एकोननवतितमः सर्गः ।। 89 ।। Thus ends the eighty-ninth sarga of Uttara Kanda of the holy Ramayana the first epic composed by sage Valmiki.
